title: What a Server Actually Does
subtitle: Listen, receive, route, process, respond — the server loop explained

sections.title: The Server Loop
sections.text:
At its core, every server runs a simple infinite loop: listen → accept → read → process → write → close. This is the fundamental pattern regardless of framework or language.

  1. Listen — Bind to a port and wait for incoming connections
  2. Accept — Accept a new TCP connection from a client
  3. Read — Read the HTTP request from the connection
  4. Process — Route the request to the appropriate handler logic
  5. Write — Send the HTTP response back to the client
  6. Close — Close the connection (or keep it alive for reuse)
sections.caption: The six-step server loop that handles every request
sections.callout: Every web framework — Express, Django, Flask, Spring, Gin — is just a wrapper around this same listen-accept-read-process-write-close loop.

sections.title: The Middleware Pipeline
sections.text:
Most web frameworks implement a middleware pipeline — a chain of functions that process the request in sequence before the final handler runs. Each middleware can:

  • Modify the request (add headers, parse body, check auth)
  • Short-circuit the chain (return a response early for auth failures)
  • Modify the response (add CORS headers, compress output)

Middleware is how servers handle cross-cutting concerns like logging, authentication, rate limiting, and CORS without cluttering the business logic.
sections.caption: Request flows through middleware chain before reaching the handler
